summary: This cluster connects through the discovery and characterization of novel algal strains from distinct aquatic environments, specifically Lake Okeechobee, Florida, and mangrove ecosystems. The alkaliphilic diatoms *Chloroidium sp. 154-1*, *Chlorella sp. 154-2*, and *Fustulifera sp. 154-3* were isolated and found to accumulate 20-30% dry weight lipids rich in palmitoleic acid (52%), palmitic acid (17%), and eicosapentaenoic acid (15%), highlighting their biofuel potential. In contrast, the newly described red alga *Bostrychia anomala* from the *Bostrychia moritziana-B. radicans* complex exhibited unique monosiphonous growth and cell-death patterns involving adjacent cell division and filament fragmentation, which may facilitate propagation in mangrove habitats. Across the cluster, research emphasizes species-specific physiological adaptations—such as alkaliphily in diatoms and novel cell death mechanisms in *Bostrychia*—that inform both biotechnological applications and ecological reproductive strategies.
